flashcards.back: Secondary storage refers to non-volatile memory devices that provide permanent data storage for computer systems. Unlike primary storage (RAM), data is retained even when power is turned off. Common examples include magnetic disks, SSDs, and optical storage devices.

flashcards.front: Define disk geometry with terms tracks, sectors, and cylinders.
flashcards.back: A track is a circular path on the disk surface where data is recorded. A sector is a wedge-shaped portion of a track that stores a fixed amount of data (typically 512 bytes). A cylinder is the set of all tracks with the same radius across all surfaces, allowing the arm to access multiple platters without moving.

flashcards.front: What is seek time in disk access?
flashcards.back: Seek time is the time required to move the disk arm to position the read/write head over the desired track. It is typically the slowest operation in disk I/O, ranging from 1 to 10 milliseconds. It includes both the time to start the arm movement and the time to settle on the correct track.

Rotational delay is the time wasted waiting for the desired sector to rotate under the read/write head. It depends on the disk's rotational speed and is typically half the time for one full rotation. For a 7200 RPM disk, average rotational delay is about 4.17 milliseconds.

flashcards.front: Define transfer time in disk operations.
flashcards.back: Transfer time is the time required to actually read or write data once the head is positioned over the correct sector. It depends on the transfer rate (bytes per second) and the amount of data being transferred. Formula: Transfer time = (bytes to transfer) / (transfer rate).

flashcards.front: What is the average disk access time formula?
flashcards.back: Average access time = Average seek time + Average rotational delay + Transfer time. This represents the typical time to read or write a random sector on disk. Modern disks have average access times of 5-10 milliseconds.

flashcards.front: What is disk scheduling and why is it needed?
flashcards.back: Disk scheduling is the technique of ordering I/O requests to minimize seek time and rotational delay. When multiple processes request disk I/O, the order of servicing these requests significantly impacts performance. Good scheduling reduces head movement and improves throughput.

flashcards.front: Describe the FCFS disk scheduling algorithm.
flashcards.back: FCFS (First-Come-First-Served) processes disk requests in the order they arrive. It is the simplest algorithm but often performs poorly because it may cause excessive head movement. No reordering of requests is performed.

flashcards.front: Describe the SSTF disk scheduling algorithm.
flashcards.back: SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) selects the request closest to the current head position, minimizing seek time between consecutive requests. It provides better performance than FCFS but may cause starvation for requests far from the current position.

flashcards.front: Describe the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm.
flashcards.back: SCAN algorithm moves the disk arm from one end to the other, servicing all requests along the way, then reverses direction. It is also called the elevator algorithm because it behaves like an elevator. It provides uniform wait times and good throughput.

flashcards.front: Describe the C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithm.
flashcards.back: C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) moves the head from one end to the other servicing requests, then jumps back to the beginning without servicing requests on the return trip. This provides more uniform wait times than SCAN by treating all tracks equally.

flashcards.front: What is low-level disk formatting (physical formatting)?
flashcards.back: Low-level formatting divides the disk into sectors and writes sector headers, error-correcting codes (ECC), and inter-sector gaps. This prepares the raw disk for use by creating the basic data structures. It is typically done at the factory and can be performed by users for repartitioning.

flashcards.front: What is partitioning in disk management?
flashcards.back: Partitioning divides a physical disk into independent sections called partitions. Each partition appears as a separate logical disk to the operating system. Partitions allow multiple operating systems and separate file systems. The partition table is stored in the Master Boot Record (MBR) or GUID Partition Table (GPT).

flashcards.front: What is logical formatting and what does it create?
flashcards.back: Logical formatting creates a file system on a partition, initializing data structures like the file allocation table (FAT), inode table, or directory entries. It marks the partition as ready to store files. This includes creating the superblock, free space management structures, and root directory.

flashcards.front: What is disk caching and how does it improve performance?
flashcards.back: Disk caching uses a portion of main memory (RAM) to store frequently accessed disk data. Since RAM access is millions of times faster than disk access, caching dramatically reduces access times for repeated reads. The cache uses algorithms like LRU to determine which blocks to keep in memory.
